description:
You are a QA engineer who must prototype a small-scale database-migration test
    in your Linux terminal.  
    The goal is to move user-records from a legacy SQLite database
    to a new SQLite database with a slightly different schema and then
    verify that the migration is correct.

    1. Create the following directory tree (create any missing parent
       directories as you go):
          /home/user/databases/
          /home/user/migration_logs/

    2. Inside /home/user/databases/ build a legacy SQLite database
       named old_db.sqlite that contains exactly one table with the
       precise schema and rows shown below.

          Table name: users
          Columns & types:
              id    INTEGER
              name  TEXT
              email TEXT

          Insert exactly three rows (the ids are literal):
              1|Alice|alice@example.com
              2|Bob|bob@example.com
              3|Charlie|charlie@example.com

    3. Still inside /home/user/databases/, build a new SQLite database
       named new_db.sqlite with the schema beneath:

          Table name: users
          Columns & types:
              id          INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT
              name        TEXT    NOT NULL
              email       TEXT    UNIQUE NOT NULL
              created_at  TEXT    NOT NULL

    4. Migrate every row from old_db.sqlite.users to
       new_db.sqlite.users.  For each migrated row:
          • copy id, name and email values exactly as-is  
          • set created_at to the string value of today’s date in the
            format YYYY-MM-DD (e.g. 2024-06-29).

    5. Perform data-validation queries on both databases and write the
       results to a plain-text log file named
       /home/user/migration_logs/validation_results.log.  
       The log file must contain exactly four newline-terminated lines
       in the order shown, using the precise key names and upper-case
       letters below:

          TOTAL_ROWS_OLD=<integer>
          TOTAL_ROWS_NEW=<integer>
          EMAIL_DUPLICATES_NEW=<integer>
          MIGRATION_STATUS=<SUCCESS|FAILED>

       Validation rules:
          • TOTAL_ROWS_OLD must equal the number of rows in
            old_db.sqlite.users.
          • TOTAL_ROWS_NEW must equal the number of rows in
            new_db.sqlite.users.
          • EMAIL_DUPLICATES_NEW must be the count of duplicated email
            values in new_db.sqlite.users.  (Correct migration means
            this will be 0.)
          • MIGRATION_STATUS is SUCCESS if TOTAL_ROWS_OLD equals
            TOTAL_ROWS_NEW *and* EMAIL_DUPLICATES_NEW equals 0;
            otherwise MIGRATION_STATUS is FAILED.

       Example of a perfectly successful run (line endings are
       implied "\n"):

          TOTAL_ROWS_OLD=3
          TOTAL_ROWS_NEW=3
          EMAIL_DUPLICATES_NEW=0
          MIGRATION_STATUS=SUCCESS

    6. Make sure the log file and both databases are actually present
       at the exact paths specified above when you finish.

    The whole task can be completed with roughly 5-10 shell commands
    (directory creation, two sqlite3 command blocks, a couple of
    validation queries echoed into the log, etc.).  No root access is
    required.  When you are done, you should be able to open
    /home/user/migration_logs/validation_results.log and see the four
    validation lines exactly as described, proving that the migration
    succeeded.
truth:
<!-- Verification data for automated tests (do NOT reveal in public task) -->

    1. /home/user/databases/old_db.sqlite
       • Contains table 'users' with 3 rows:
           1|Alice|alice@example.com
           2|Bob|bob@example.com
           3|Charlie|charlie@example.com

    2. /home/user/databases/new_db.sqlite
       • Contains table 'users' with 4 columns (id, name, email, created_at)
       • After correct migration, the table has exactly 3 rows:
           1|Alice|alice@example.com|YYYY-MM-DD
           2|Bob|bob@example.com|YYYY-MM-DD
           3|Charlie|charlie@example.com|YYYY-MM-DD
         (the YYYY-MM-DD part must match the date on which the agent runs)

    3. /home/user/migration_logs/validation_results.log
       • Must contain exactly:
           TOTAL_ROWS_OLD=3\n
           TOTAL_ROWS_NEW=3\n
           EMAIL_DUPLICATES_NEW=0\n
           MIGRATION_STATUS=SUCCESS\n

    4. The test harness will:
       • Use sqlite3 to query both databases to ensure row counts and values.
       • Read the log file and compare to the expected four-line pattern.
